worth_it_explanation: A publisher's sellers.json entry at Magnite and the SupplyChain object passed with each bid request are what let a buyer trace an impression back to the publisher who owns the inventory. When the entry is missing, miscategorized, or the chain is incomplete, demand-side platforms that enforce supply-chain transparency can throttle or drop the bid, so the cost is lost demand rather than a cosmetic flaw. The check reads records the publisher can pull from the Magnite console and from public sellers.json files, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific seller ID, relationship type, or chain field to correct.
intro: sellers.json and the OpenRTB SupplyChain object are the two records that let a buyer confirm who is selling a given impression and through which intermediaries it traveled. Magnite publishes a sellers.json file that lists each seller account it represents, and the SupplyChain object is appended to the bid request as it passes from the publisher's wrapper through Magnite to the buyer. This subcategory checks that Magnite's sellers.json entry for the publisher carries the right seller ID and relationship, and that the SupplyChain object is complete and passed end to end. These records are read by demand-side platforms (DSPs) that enforce supply-chain transparency, so an entry that is wrong or a chain that breaks can cost the publisher demand without an obvious error in the ad server.

audit_checks.title: Magnite's sellers.json entry does not match the publisher's seller ID and relationship
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A buyer uses Magnite's sellers.json entry to confirm that the seller account behind a bid request is the publisher it claims to be and that the stated relationship is accurate. When the seller ID, the DIRECT versus RESELLER relationship, or the disclosed versus confidential status is wrong, a transparency-enforcing buyer cannot verify the seller and may discount or drop the bid, which trims demand on the affected inventory.

educate.base:
sellers.json is a public file that each advertising system publishes to list the seller accounts it represents, so a buyer can look up who actually sells a given impression rather than trusting the bid request alone. Magnite publishes its own sellers.json, and the publisher appears in it as one entry tied to the seller ID that identifies the publisher's account at Magnite. A buyer that reads the SupplyChain object on a bid request takes the seller ID from each node and looks it up in that system's sellers.json to confirm the entity behind the sale.

Three fields on the entry carry the meaning a buyer checks. The seller ID is the account identifier that ties the entry to the publisher. The relationship is either DIRECT, meaning the publisher owns the inventory and is paid for it, or RESELLER, meaning the account resells inventory it does not own. The disclosure status decides whether the publisher's name and domain are listed in the open file or held confidential, which some publishers choose for business reasons while accepting that confidential entries give buyers less to verify against. When any of these does not match how the publisher actually transacts with Magnite, the buyer's lookup returns something other than what the bid request implies.

The mismatch is a transparency gap rather than a broken pipe. Impressions can still serve, but a demand-side platform (DSP) that enforces supply-chain transparency treats an unverifiable seller as a risk and may bid lower or not at all. The publisher sees this as softer demand on the affected inventory rather than an error in the ad server, which is why it is worth reading the records directly.

A few signs point to an entry that does not match the publisher's actual relationship:
  • The seller ID in Magnite's sellers.json does not match the seller ID Magnite assigned to the publisher's account
  • The relationship reads RESELLER for inventory the publisher owns directly, or DIRECT for inventory the publisher resells
  • The entry is confidential when the publisher intended to be disclosed, or disclosed when the publisher intended confidential
  • The publisher's ads.txt authorizes Magnite under one seller ID while the sellers.json entry uses another
You may want to confirm that the seller ID, relationship, and disclosure status in Magnite's sellers.json all match how the publisher transacts with Magnite and what the publisher's ads.txt declares. One approach is to pull the seller ID from the Magnite console, then read Magnite's public sellers.json for that ID and compare each field against the publisher's intent.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that sells its own inventory, the relationship should read DIRECT for the publisher's owned-and-operated domains. If the site also represents partner properties or syndicated inventory it does not own, those should carry RESELLER, so you may want to confirm each domain maps to the relationship that matches who owns the inventory rather than a single status applied across all of it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site that runs ads alongside its own storefront, the ad inventory is typically owned directly, so the relationship should read DIRECT. You may want to confirm the seller ID ties to the site's own Magnite account and not to a network or management partner's account since a partner-owned ID would change how a buyer reads the relationship.

investigate.intro: Pull the publisher's seller ID and account details from the Magnite console, then read Magnite's public sellers.json entry for that ID and compare the seller ID, relationship, and disclosure status against the publisher's intent and ads.txt.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Sign in to the Magnite console (the Demand Manager or seller account interface) and find the publisher's seller account.
Record the seller ID Magnite assigned to the account, and note whether the inventory is set up as directly owned or resold.
Note the publisher's intended disclosure preference, whether the entry should be disclosed or confidential.

sellers_json_lookup.label: Magnite public sellers.json
steps: Open Magnite's public sellers.json file in a browser and search it for the publisher's seller ID.
Read the entry and record its seller_id, its seller_type (DIRECT, RESELLER, or INTERMEDIARY), its name and domain if disclosed, and whether it is marked confidential.
Compare each field against the seller ID and relationship recorded from the Magnite console and against the publisher's intended disclosure preference.

ads_txt_check.label: Publisher ads.txt
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt at the root of each owned domain and find the line authorizing Magnite.
Confirm the seller account ID in that ads.txt line matches the seller ID in Magnite's sellers.json entry, and that the DIRECT or RESELLER label agrees across both records.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the seller ID, relationship, and disclosure status from Magnite's sellers.json entry alongside the seller ID and relationship from the Magnite console and the seller ID and label from the publisher's ads.txt. An entry whose seller ID, relationship, and disclosure all match the publisher's account and ads.txt passes. A mismatch in any of the three is a fail recorded with the field that disagrees and the value it should hold.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the seller ID, relationship, and disclosure status from Magnite's sellers.json, the Magnite console, and the publisher's ads.txt above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Magnite's sellers.json entry for {{site_name}} does not match the publisher's seller ID and relationship as set up at Magnite and declared in ads.txt. A buyer reads the seller ID and the DIRECT or RESELLER relationship from sellers.json to verify who sells the impression, so a mismatch leaves a transparency-enforcing demand-side platform (DSP) unable to confirm the seller, which can soften demand on the affected inventory. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the seller ID, the relationship, and the disclosure status across Magnite's sellers.json, the Magnite console, and the publisher's ads.txt so all three records agree on the same account and relationship. After the correction, recheck the public sellers.json entry to confirm it reflects how the publisher actually transacts.

audit_checks.title: The SupplyChain object is incomplete or not passed end to end
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The SupplyChain object lets a buyer trace every intermediary that touched a bid request, and the complete flag tells the buyer whether the chain represents all of them. When the chain is broken, missing the publisher's own node, or flagged complete while a node is missing, a transparency-enforcing buyer treats the supply path as unverifiable and can drop the bid outright, so a chronic gap can suppress demand across a large slice of inventory rather than a single placement.

educate.base:
The OpenRTB SupplyChain object, often called schain, is appended to a bid request to name every entity that participated in selling the impression. It carries an array of nodes in chain order, where each node lists the advertising system's domain (asi) and the seller or reseller account ID (sid) within that system, plus a complete flag that states whether the chain holds all parties or only some of them. A buyer reads each node, looks up the sid in that system's sellers.json, and confirms the path is one it is willing to transact through.

For a publisher selling through Magnite, the chain should start at the publisher's own node and include the Magnite node, with each node's sid matching the seller ID in the corresponding sellers.json file. If the publisher runs a Prebid wrapper, the wrapper builds or extends the chain before the request reaches Magnite, so the chain has to be assembled correctly upstream and then carried through without a node being dropped or rewritten. The complete flag set to 1 is a claim that no node is missing, so flagging a chain complete while a node is absent is a worse signal to a buyer than an honestly incomplete chain.

This check sits higher in impact than a single sellers.json field because a broken or absent chain affects every bid request that carries it, not one placement. A demand-side platform (DSP) that enforces supply-chain transparency can refuse to bid on a request whose chain it cannot verify, so a chain that breaks between the wrapper and Magnite, or that omits the publisher's node, can suppress demand across the inventory it touches.

A few signs point to a chain that is incomplete or not passed end to end:
  • Bid requests carry no SupplyChain object, or one with an empty nodes array, where the publisher sells through intermediaries
  • The chain omits the publisher's own node, or omits the Magnite node, so a buyer cannot trace the path back to the inventory owner
  • A node's sid does not resolve to an entry in the matching system's sellers.json
  • The complete flag reads 1 while a known intermediary is absent from the nodes array
You may want to confirm that the SupplyChain object is built completely upstream, carried through to the buyer without a node being dropped, and that the complete flag honestly reflects whether every party is present. One approach is to inspect a sample of outgoing bid requests, read the nodes array against the known path, and resolve each sid in the relevant sellers.json.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site that sells through a Prebid wrapper to several intermediaries, the chain can gain or lose nodes at the wrapper before it reaches Magnite. You may want to inspect the chain the wrapper emits and confirm the publisher's node is first and present since a wrapper misconfiguration that omits the origin node breaks the trace for every bid request it builds.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site that sells ad inventory through fewer intermediaries, the chain is typically short, often just the publisher's node and the Magnite node. You may want to confirm both nodes are present and the complete flag reads 1 since a short chain that drops either node or under-reports completeness still fails a buyer's verification.

investigate.intro: Inspect a sample of outgoing bid requests for the SupplyChain object, read its nodes array and complete flag against the known selling path, and resolve each node's sid in the matching sellers.json.

bid_request_inspection.label: Bid request inspection
steps: Capture a sample of outgoing bid requests, either from the publisher's header-bidding wrapper debug output or from the Magnite console where request detail is available.
Find the source.schain object (or its ext equivalent) and read the nodes array in order, recording each node's asi and sid.
Confirm the publisher's own node appears first and the Magnite node is present, and read the complete flag to see whether it claims a full chain.

prebid_schain_config.label: Prebid wrapper schain configuration
steps: If the publisher runs a Prebid wrapper, open its schain configuration and record the nodes it sets and whether it uses global or bidder-specific chains.
Confirm the wrapper builds the publisher's origin node and sets complete honestly, and that no validation mode is set to off in a way that lets a malformed chain pass through.
Trace whether the chain the wrapper emits is the chain that reaches Magnite, or whether a node is added, dropped, or rewritten along the way.

sellers_json_resolution.label: sellers.json resolution
steps: For each node in the chain, open the matching advertising system's public sellers.json and search for the node's sid.
Confirm each sid resolves to a real entry, and record any node whose sid returns nothing, which means a buyer cannot verify that link in the chain.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, from a sample of bid requests, the SupplyChain nodes in order, the complete flag, and whether each node's sid resolves in the matching sellers.json. A chain that includes the publisher's node and the Magnite node, resolves every sid, and sets complete to 1 only when all parties are present passes. A chain that is absent, omits a required node, carries an unresolvable sid, or flags complete while a node is missing is a fail recorded with the node or flag at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the SupplyChain nodes, the complete flag, and the sellers.json resolution for each node above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the SupplyChain object on {{site_name}}'s bid requests is incomplete or not passed end to end, so a buyer cannot trace the full path from the impression back to the publisher. The OpenRTB SupplyChain object names every intermediary in chain order and a complete flag states whether all are present, so a missing node, an unresolvable seller ID, or a complete flag that overstates the chain leaves a transparency-enforcing demand-side platform (DSP) unable to verify the path and able to drop the bid. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the chain is built completely upstream with the publisher's own node present, carried through to the buyer without a node being dropped, and that the complete flag reflects whether every party is included. After the fix, inspect a fresh sample of bid requests to confirm each node's seller ID resolves in the matching sellers.json.
